query: Ethel Winter worked with which avant-garde theater director?When ready, output the final answer enclosed in <answer> and </answer> tags. Do not generate any content after the </answer> tag.
query_analysis:
Concise Summary: The query asks for the specific avant-garde theater director associated with Ethel Winter, requiring factual retrieval from historical or biographical sources.

Required Skills:
Biographical research, entity linking (connecting individuals to professional roles), and verification of historical associations.

Relevant Tools:
Wikipedia_RAG_Search_Tool is the primary choice for retrieving structured biographical data on Ethel Winter and her collaborators; Ground_Google_Search_Tool serves as a secondary option for cross-referencing recent or non-Wikipedia sources.

Additional Considerations:
Ensure the search query explicitly mentions 'avant-garde' to filter relevant directors, as Ethel Winter may have worked with multiple figures; verify the specific nature of the collaboration if multiple directors are found.

Winter was a leading member of the Martha Graham Dance Company from the 1940s through the 1960s, creating roles in many of Graham's works and often performing roles originally choreographed for Graham herself[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ethel_Winter), [2](https://www.patandmeloakes.com/EthelWinter.html), [3](https://dance.washington.edu/people/ethel-winter), [4](https://www.ibdb.com/broadway-cast-staff/ethel-winter-103179).

While primarily associated with modern dance, which was considered avant-garde for its time, the provided information does not explicitly state that Ethel Winter worked with other avant-garde *theater* directors outside of the dance realm. Paul Taylor and Merce Cunningham, both significant figures in avant-garde dance, also had connections to the Martha Graham company, with Taylor having danced alongside Winter, but there is no indication that Winter worked *with* them as a director in an avant-garde theatrical capacity[2](https://www.patandmeloakes.com/EthelWinter.html), [6](https://www.artsjournal.com/dancebeat/2012/03/re-entering-marthas-inner-landscapes/), [7](https://yaledancelab.yale.edu/the-legacy-of-merce-cunningham), [8](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Merce_Cunningham), [9](https://www.beyondthenotes.org/blog/merce-cunningham-1919-2009-dancer-choreographer-and-interdisciplinary-collaborator/), [10](https://www.merce.org/), [11](https://paultaylordance.org/leadership/paul-taylor/)."]}}

result:
Ethel Winter, a prominent American modern dancer, worked extensively with Martha Graham, a highly influential and avant-garde figure in the world of dance. Winter was a leading member of the Martha Graham Dance Company from the 1940s through the 1960s, creating roles in many of Graham's works and often performing roles originally choreographed for Graham herself[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ethel_Winter), [2](https://www.patandmeloakes.com/EthelWinter.html), [3](https://dance.washington.edu/people/ethel-winter), [4](https://www.ibdb.com/broadway-cast-staff/ethel-winter-103179).

While primarily associated with modern dance, which was considered avant-garde for its time, the provided information does not explicitly state that Ethel Winter worked with other avant-garde *theater* directors outside of the dance realm. She also performed with companies led by other dancers from the Graham lineage, such as Sophie Maslow and Anna Sokolow[2](https://www.patandmeloakes.com/EthelWinter.html), [5](https://archive.batsheva.co.il/en/people/ethel-winter/). Paul Taylor and Merce Cunningham, both significant figures in avant-garde dance, also had connections to the Martha Graham company, with Taylor having danced alongside Winter, but there is no indication that Winter worked *with* them as a director in an avant-garde theatrical capacity[2](https://www.patandmeloakes.com/EthelWinter.html), [6](https://www.artsjournal.com/dancebeat/2012/03/re-entering-marthas-inner-landscapes/), [7](https://yaledancelab.yale.edu/the-legacy-of-merce-cunningham), [8](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Merce_Cunningham), [9](https://www.beyondthenotes.org/blog/merce-cunningham-1919-2009-dancer-choreographer-and-interdisciplinary-collaborator/), [10](https://www.merce.org/), [11](https://paultaylordance.org/leadership/paul-taylor/).
